label: General context — not the calibrated cost model.

concepts.definition: Rapid re-housing moves people out of homelessness quickly by combining short-term rental assistance with housing-search and stabilization services. It does not require people to be 'housing ready' first — housing comes first, support follows.

Permanent supportive housing (PSH) pairs long-term, non-time-limited rental housing with voluntary wrap-around services (health, mental health, case management). It targets people with chronic homelessness and disabling conditions, and is the most service-intensive intervention.

Prevention helps people at imminent risk of homelessness keep their current housing or move directly to new housing — through short-term financial help, mediation, or legal aid — before they ever enter shelter or the street.

The Point-in-Time (PIT) count is a HUD-required count of sheltered and unsheltered people experiencing homelessness on a single night in January each year. It is the headline measure of how many people are homeless in a community.

source.title: HUD Exchange — PIT and HIC Guides

concepts.definition: A Continuum of Care (CoC) is the regional planning body HUD funds to coordinate homelessness services for a defined geography (a city, county, or region). CA-600 is the Los Angeles City & County CoC. WaitCost answers at the CoC level — never about individuals.

HUD defines chronic homelessness as homelessness lasting a year or more (or repeated episodes totaling a year over three years) for a person with a disabling condition. This group has the highest public costs and is the primary target of permanent supportive housing.

Across cities, the cost of housing is the strongest predictor of how many people become homeless — stronger than poverty or unemployment. In WaitCost's learned inflow model, median home value is the dominant SHAP driver, matching the research that homelessness is primarily a housing-affordability problem.

source.title: Colburn & Aldern, Homelessness Is a Housing Problem (2022)
